title: Collaboration & Open Theses
text:
I currently do not have any open thesis topics advertised.
However, for strong and motivated students, I can often arrange a suitable project. If you are interested in working with me, please have a look at my Research & Publications as well as my past and ongoing supervised theses, and propose what you would like to work on.
You can also find thesis openings at our lab on the EBC student theses page.
For concrete project ideas, thesis proposals, or collaboration inquiries, please contact me by email.

title: Collaboration & Open Theses
text:
Expected Background
Useful prior knowledge includes control theory, optimization, linear systems, numerical methods, thermodynamics, energy systems, and programming, preferably in Python.

title: Collaboration & Open Theses
text:
How to Apply
Send a short email including:

Which research direction you are interested in
Your study program and semester
Relevant courses
Prior thesis/project experience
A current transcript

title: Research & Publications
text: My research is concerned with machine learning, prediction, control, and the intersections of these fields. My methodological focus is on data-driven and predictive control approaches for technical energy systems.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Research Interests

Model predictive control
Data-enabled predictive control
Reinforcement learning controllers
Data-driven modeling and prediction
Distributed control of energy systems

title: Research & Publications
text:
Methodological Directions
One direction I am currently highly interested in is prediction and control based on time series foundation models. I would like to understand how these models can be used not only for forecasting, but also as useful components in predictive control, and anomaly detection.
Another core interest is making complex nonlinear controllers available on inexpensive and resource-constrained hardware. Neural controllers and approximate MPC are promising tools for distilling computationally demanding controllers into fast policies.
I am also interested in the overlap between MPC, model predictive path integral control, model-based reinforcement learning, and model-free reinforcement learning. These fields often address similar problems with different assumptions and computational trade-offs.
For distributed energy systems, I would like to investigate primal-dual schemes for efficient prosumer markets in low-temperature district heating networks. More broadly, I am interested in agentic distributed control: making complex control systems configurable from smaller interacting subunits, so that larger systems can be designed, adapted, and operated in a modular way.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Application Areas


My application interests are energy-related. I am especially interested in:

Energy grids, especially low-temperature district heating networks
Heating, cooling, and power generation systems
Heat pumps, refrigeration cycles, and related supply machinery
Energy hubs and coupled heat and power supply systems
Industrial waste heat integration

title: Research & Publications
text:
Projects

PAALi
Process-optimized extraction of waste heat from the food industry into innovative 4th and 5th generation heating networks in two districts of the municipalities of Zapfendorf and Hagenow
I am involved in the PAALi project, a publicly funded joint project that investigates how waste heat from the food industry can be integrated into the heat supply of rural districts. The project focuses on previously unused low-temperature waste heat at the BMi plant in Zapfendorf and the Kühne site in Hagenow, with the goal of supporting a regional heat transition.
A central part of PAALi is the development of an intelligent transfer station for heat extraction. Model-based methods and simulation studies are used to represent thermal processes, develop control strategies, and evaluate operating and expansion scenarios for low-temperature heating networks. Pilot plants at both industrial sites provide operational data that connect the modeling and optimization work with practical implementation.
More details are available on the PAALi project page.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Publications
I will keep my publication list active here.

Distributed Nonlinear Model Predictive Control for Building Energy Systems: An ALADIN Implementation Study


Authors: Steffen Eser, Ben Spoek, Augustinus Schütz, Phillip Stoffel, Dirk Müller


Journal: Energy and AI, Volume 21, 2025, Article 100536


Link: ScienceDirect




Abstract


The implementation of sophisticated control strategies for building energy systems is crucial for improving energy efficiency and occupant comfort. While nonlinear model predictive control offers promising benefits, its application to large-scale building systems remains challenging due to computational complexity and system coupling. This work presents a comprehensive study of nonlinear distributed model predictive control implementation for building energy systems, comparing Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers and Augmented Lagrangian Alternating Direction Inexact Newton algorithms alongside different modeling approaches.


We examine a multi-zone heating system with thermal storage and multiple producers, investigating both ordinary differential equation based and artificial neural network based modeling strategies. Through systematic parameter tuning using Bayesian optimization and closed-loop scaling analysis with up to 40 thermal zones, we demonstrate that ALADIN-based nonlinear distributed model predictive control can achieve performance comparable to centralized model predictive control, showing greater robustness to parameter variations than ADMM.


Our results reveal that artificial neural network based models effectively mitigate distributed integration errors and significantly reduce computation time compared to ordinary differential equation based approaches. Detailed computational profiling identifies specific bottlenecks in different nonlinear distributed model predictive control components. These findings advance the practical implementation of nonlinear distributed model predictive control in building energy systems, offering concrete strategies for modeling choices, parameter tuning, and system architecture design.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Own Theses and Project Work

Master’s Thesis: An ALADIN-Based Approach for Data-Driven Distributed Model Predictive Control


Supervisor: Steffen Esser




Summary


Improving building energy systems control offers a key to reducing greenhouse gas emissions while balancing energy efficiency and comfort. Model predictive control is a promising approach, particularly for managing complex building energy systems holistically. However, this entails challenging non-convex optimization problems with high computational requirements that scale with system size. Distributed MPC can mitigate this through parallel subproblem solving, but requires efficient algorithms. The Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers shows limited success due to sublinear convergence in non-convex scenarios, whereas the Augmented Lagrangian Alternating Direction Inexact Newton algorithm promises faster convergence but faces practical challenges in application and parameterization.


This thesis adopts two strategies to address these challenges: a data-driven approach using artificial neural networks for rapid, comprehensive system analysis, and an automated parameterization technique to improve performance. Tests on a building energy system show that the proposed MPC reduces operating costs by 15.7% compared with ADMM and outperforms the centralized solver IPOPT in computational efficiency for larger systems, without compromising control quality. Artificial neural network models further reduce computation times significantly, demonstrating the potential of ALADIN-based MPC for efficient and stable building energy systems control while maintaining comfort standards.




Bachelor’s Thesis: Development and Evaluation of Machine Learning Methods for Dynamic Defrost Control of Air-to-Water Heat Pumps Regarding Adaptability and Transferability


Supervisor: Dr. Jonas Klingebiel




Summary


During the operation of air-to-water heat pumps, frost can form on the evaporator fins depending on ambient conditions, reducing heat transfer and lowering efficiency. The evaporator must therefore be defrosted regularly, but the energy used for defrosting is a loss that should be minimized. Commercial defrost controllers often initiate defrosting cyclically at fixed time intervals, even though frost growth depends strongly on ambient conditions and the heat pump’s operating mode. As a result, defrosting is often initiated at a suboptimal time.


In this thesis, machine learning methods are used to develop self-learning defrosting controllers that approximate the optimal time for defrost initiation and thereby increase efficiency. The work investigates the ability of these controllers to adapt to disturbances such as foliage on the evaporator housing and technical changes to the heat pump, such as modified evaporator geometries. Two reinforcement learning agents, PPO and DQN, are implemented with hyperparameter optimization and compared. The results show that self-learning controls improve efficiency compared with time-based methods and that the reinforcement learning agents are capable of generalization, following reasonable defrosting strategies even under changing conditions.




Project Work: Integration of Power-to-Gas Technologies in the Multi-Objective Operational Optimization of Energy Systems


Supervisor: Yifan Wang




Summary


To reduce CO2 emissions and reach climate targets, many nations are turning to electricity generation from renewable sources. Since solar radiation and wind cannot be controlled but should be used as much as possible, power peaks in the electricity grid can occur. The increasing share of renewable energies such as wind and solar power therefore calls for flexible balancing of the electricity grid. In addition, sectors such as aviation require indirect electrification.


Power-to-gas is a promising future technology for both challenges. The start-up times of power-to-gas plants are very short, making them suitable for grid balancing, and the generated fuel gases can be used as climate-neutral fuels if the required electricity comes from renewable sources. In this project work, the components electrolyzer, direct air capture carbon separator, and methanizer were modeled and incorporated into the simulation model of a power system with a dynamic load profile and variable electricity prices.


The suitability of power-to-gas plants for grid balancing was demonstrated. The work also showed that downtime of power-to-gas plants decreases significantly with an increasing share of renewable energies in the power grid, suggesting that prices for fuel gases generated in this way may decline in the future as renewable shares continue to rise.

title: Hobbies & Interests
text:
I am passionate about running, cycling, triathlon, and recently also HYROX. Endurance sport is a great balance to my academic work, and lessons from one often carry over into the other.
I also really enjoy mountain and water sports. I am a certified trainer in multiple sports, because I love sharing my passion for sport with others.
Outside of work and sports, I enjoy making pizza in my pizza oven, preparing good espresso, and spending time with friends.

title: Privacy Notice
text:
Hosting
This website is hosted with GitHub Pages. When you visit the website, GitHub may process technical access data such as your IP address, request time, requested files, browser information, and referrer information in server logs. This processing is necessary to deliver the website and to maintain the security and reliability of the hosting service.
More information is available in the GitHub Privacy Statement.

title: Privacy Notice
text:
No Analytics or Tracking
This website does not use analytics tools, tracking cookies, contact forms, comments, newsletters, or embedded social media widgets.

title: Privacy Notice

Email Contact
If you contact me by email, I process the information you provide in order to answer your request. The data are not used for automated decision-making and are not passed on unless this is necessary to handle your request or required by law.

title: Teaching
text:
Supervised Student Theses
My students are my team. I rely on them heavily for developing new ideas and for bringing new perspectives and inspiration into my work. For that reason, good supervision is very important to me.
I also want to encourage a team spirit among my students. In addition to student activities organized at the institute, such as the summer BBQ, Christmas party, and Christmas market event, I have lunch with my students every two weeks. This gives everyone time to connect, get to know each other, and talk about topics beyond day-to-day work.

Stochastic Model Predictive Control for Consumer-Driven District Heating Networks


Student: Lara Koppel


Status: Finished


Supervisors: Ben Spoek, M.Sc.; Dr. Sebastian Stemmler


Auditor: Prof. Dr.-Ing. Dirk Müller




Description


The heating sector accounts for a large part of global energy consumption and remains heavily dependent on fossil fuels. To achieve climate neutrality, a transition toward modern district heating networks with reduced temperatures is required, as these networks enable the integration of distributed renewable energy and urban waste heat. Efficiently managing these systems is complex due to the dynamic interactions between distributed consumers and centralized producers. This complexity requires advanced control strategies such as model predictive control. Deploying these predictive architectures is challenging, as prolonged hydraulic transport delays lead to the accumulation of temporally correlated weather forecast errors.


This work proposes a demand-side stochastic model predictive control framework and evaluates it against a deterministic baseline. The study focuses on decentralized building control by abstracting the network interface into a constant transport delay, placing supply-side dispatch and detailed hydraulic network dynamics outside the scope. To handle uncertainty, the disturbance model integrates Ensemble Model Output Statistics with a first-order autoregressive error model. This generates calibrated probabilistic forecasts that capture the autocorrelation of prediction errors. An augmented state-space formulation propagates the forecast error variance via closed-form linear covariance equations. This enables probabilistic constraint adaptation, dynamically replacing static safety margins.


Closed-loop control simulations across varying network transport delays, building types, and seasonal conditions indicate that the operational advantage over the certainty-equivalence deterministic baseline is governed by the variance of the state covariance matrix. This advantage is amplified by the extension of the hydraulic transport horizon, meteorological input volatility, and the speed of the building’s transient thermal response. Under amplifying conditions, relative energy savings of up to 43.7% are achieved compared to the deterministic benchmark at equivalent comfort constraint satisfaction. The results suggest that uncertainty-aware demand-side control is particularly beneficial for thermally sensitive buildings with limited passive thermal buffering.




Consumer-Driven Decentralized Model Predictive Control for District Heating Applications


Student: Jaspar Paulus


Status: Finished


Supervisors: Ben Spoek, M.Sc.


Auditor: Prof. Dr.-Ing. Dirk Müller




Description


Decarbonizing the building heat sector is a prerequisite for achieving the greenhouse gas reduction targets of the Paris Climate Agreement. District heating networks offer a promising pathway, enabling the large-scale integration of renewable and waste heat sources while leveraging economies of scale in heat generation. Realizing this potential requires control strategies that go beyond conventional rule-based control, which cannot fully exploit the thermal flexibility of the network and the connected buildings.


Model predictive control (MPC) offers a principled framework for demand-oriented, efficiency-optimized district heating operation. Centralized approaches that jointly optimize the entire system are, however, computationally intractable for real-world systems due to the high dimensionality and nonlinear dynamics of the coupled network-consumer system. This thesis proposes and investigates a novel consumer-driven, decentralized two-level MPC architecture. Each consumer employs an individual MPC to schedule its own heat demand predictively while adhering to comfort constraints and cost considerations. The consolidated demand forecasts are coordinated by a single producer MPC, which determines the optimal thermal energy input trajectory at the energy hub to satisfy all consumer demands simultaneously, accounting for transport delays, thermal losses, and the thermal inertia of the distribution network.


To evaluate this concept, a complete simulation environment is developed from scratch in Python. It encompasses a white-box Plug Flow Pipe network model capturing transport delay and thermal losses; a data-driven autoregressive model with exogenous inputs pipe model serving as the producer MPC prediction model; a white-box thermal building model coupled with a consumer MPC; a producer MPC that translates the sum of consumer heat orders into an optimized supply temperature trajectory; and a classical proportional-integral controlled reference system for comparison. The proposed system is evaluated on a simplified two-district network, each district comprising four thermal zones, which collectively equate to approximately thirty single-family houses, over two representative one-week simulation periods in winter and spring, each investigated under dual and single night-setback configurations.


Within the scope of this study, the results demonstrate various advantages of the consumer-driven MPC over the proportional-integral reference. Accumulated thermal comfort violations are reduced by approximately 73% in spring and 81% in winter. Network heat losses decrease by 5% to 8% in spring and by 16% to 17% in winter, reducing the total energy input at the energy hub by 1.3% to 2.3% in spring and 2.7% to 2.9% in winter, respectively.




Off-Design Operation of Heat Pumps in Energy Hubs: Analysis of the Interactions Between Heat Pump Design and System Design


Student: Peter Nölke


Status: Finished


Supervisors: Sebastian Ostlender, M.Sc.; Ben Spoek, M.Sc.


Auditor: Prof. Dr.-Ing. Dirk Müller




Description


In order to reduce greenhouse gas emissions in central heating supply, fossil fuels must be replaced by renewable energy sources. One way of doing this is to integrate electrically powered heat pumps into energy hubs. The operating behavior of heat pumps is heavily dependent on the operating point, which is referred to as off-design behavior. In off-design operation, both the efficiency and the available condenser power vary. The effects of this off-design behavior on the operation of energy hubs have not yet been comprehensively analyzed.


In this thesis, a dynamic simulation model of an energy hub with 1.3 MW total thermal capacity is developed based on validated sub-models. This enables a holistic view of the effects of off-design behavior on the energy hub. The model includes a heat pump as the primary energy generator and a combined heat and power plant as the secondary energy generator. The off-design behavior of the heat pump is mapped using a detailed map model that describes the condenser power and the efficiency of the heat pump as a function of the operating point and design parameters. The design parameters of the heat pump are the design point, the refrigerant, and the flowsheet. For comparison, a simplified model is used that maps the operating behavior using the Carnot efficiency and does not take any design parameters into account. Both approaches are analyzed in annual simulations with varying design capacities of the heat pump and combined heat and power unit. The simulation results are evaluated using the seasonal performance factor of the heat pump, the overall efficiency of the energy hub, and the proportion of renewable thermal energy.


The results show that both the seasonal performance factor and the overall efficiency depend on the power output of the heat pump. The magnitude of this influence depends on the combination of design parameters. Depending on the design capacity of the heat pump, combinations with different emphases should be selected. For small heat pump design capacity, the focus is on a large peak power of the condenser in order to achieve a high share of renewable thermal energy. For large heat pump design capacity, the seasonal performance factor becomes more important, as it contributes significantly to optimizing the overall efficiency of the energy hub.


This work demonstrates that knowledge of the detailed off-design behavior of the heat pump is relevant for system design. In order to realize potential energy savings, the design parameters of the heat pump must be taken into account in the design.




Subspace Identification Methods in Anomaly Detection of Buildings


Student: Dana Meier


Status: Finished


Supervisors: Dr. Efe Balta; Ben Spoek, M.Sc.; Dr. Volkher Scholz; Remo Pfister


Auditor: Prof. Dr. John Lygeros
